insights: All five incident categories have exactly 100 assigned incidents each, reflecting a perfectly uniform distribution with no variation across categories.
The standard deviation of incident counts across categories is 0.00 and the range (max − min) is 0, confirming the absence of any statistical discrepancy or concentration.
There is no evidence of category-level imbalance in incident assignment—neither over- nor under-representation exists for any category.

summary: The analysis directly addresses the goal of identifying discrepancies or imbalances in incident distribution across categories and finds none: all five categories contain exactly 100 incidents (20.0% share each), with zero variance in counts. This perfect uniformity implies highly controlled or rule-based assignment logic, eliminating concerns about category-specific overload, underutilization, or bias in routing. As no structural imbalance exists, further investigation into category-level resource allocation, prioritization, or root cause disparities is not warranted. Next focus should shift to other dimensions—such as time, assignee, severity, or resolution outcome—where meaningful variation may exist.
